commonMistakes: Suppressing the dependency linter instead of fixing the data flow.
Using an Effect for render-time derivation.
Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain mount, update, and unmount using a new example and justify the result.
Explain effects as synchronization using a new example and justify the result.
Choose and justify when an effect is appropriate using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Mount, update, and unmount** → **Effects as synchronization** → **When an effect is appropriate**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Mount, update, and unmount.
choices.label: Ask whether logic is caused by an event, derivable during render, or synchronizing with an external system. Only the last generally needs an Effect.

choices.feedback: Mount, update, and unmount is best understood as follows: A component mounts when added, updates after later renders and commits, and unmounts when removed.
choices.label: A component mounts when added, updates after later renders and commits, and unmounts when removed.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Mount, update, and unmount.
choices.label: Choose by ownership, consumers, update frequency, persistence, and source of truth. Context, Redux, and server-state libraries solve different problems.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Mount, update, and unmount.
choices.label: A trigger causes render, React computes an element tree, reconciles it with the previous tree, and commits necessary host changes.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of When an effect is appropriate.
choices.label: Ask whether logic is caused by an event, derivable during render, or synchronizing with an external system. Only the last generally needs an Effect.

choices.feedback: When an effect is appropriate is best understood as follows: Use an Effect for synchronization caused by rendering. Derived calculations belong in render and action-specific logic belongs in event handlers.
choices.label: Use an Effect for synchronization caused by rendering. Derived calculations belong in render and action-specific logic belongs in event handlers.

choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
choices.label: Apply Mount, update, and unmount deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Mount, update, and unmount, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.
choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.
choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.
choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.
questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Component Lifecycle and Effects?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.
choices.label: Avoid Component Lifecycle and Effects completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.
choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Component Lifecycle and Effects.
choices.label: Use Component Lifecycle and Effects in every file because more abstraction is always better.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use Component Lifecycle and Effects when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Component Lifecycle and Effects?

summary: Component Lifecycle and Effects connects Mount, update, and unmount, Effects as synchronization, When an effect is appropriate.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: Component Lifecycle and Effects

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Component Lifecycle and Effects is part of the mental model learners need before making reliable implementation decisions. It belongs to the **Effects and Hooks** module.
